calculator.withSeparationDesc: Check if you physically cut edge connectors (ISA/PCI/RAM) and remove gold-plated ICs before acid immersion — only ~8–10% of PCB mass goes into the bath, reducing reagent use by up to 10×. Metal yield remains unchanged.
calculator.separationActive: Separation active — chemical volumes calculated for connector fraction (~8–10% of feed mass)
calculator.reagentPricesDesc: Values saved locally and used in chemistry cost calculation
calculator.noMetalsRecovered: No metals recovered from this process

processes.title: Chemical Process Database
processes.subtitle: Technical specifications of recovery processes
processes.estimatedDesc: Reagent quantities, process times, and recovery yields are indicative — they depend on the specific material, acid concentrations, temperature, and laboratory conditions. Data is for informational and educational purposes only.
processes.disclaimer: Estimated values. Reagent quantities, process times, and recovery yields are indicative — they depend on the specific material, acid concentrations, temperature, and laboratory conditions. Data is for informational and educational purposes only.
processes.selectMaterial: — select material to see estimated recovery —
processes.estimatedRecoveryNote: *Estimate based on typical values — actual recovery may vary
processes.aquaRegiaWarning: BEFORE process: Pre-etch feed in dilute HNO3 (25–30%, 40°C, 1–2 h/kg) to remove Cu, Zn, Ni and base metals. Skipping this reduces Au recovery purity.

descriptions.aqua_regia: Classic two-stage Au/Ag/PGM recovery from e-waste. Stage 1: HNO3 25% pre-etch removes base metals (Cu, Ni, Pb) and dissolves Ag. Stage 2: Aqua Regia (HCl:HNO3 3:1) dissolves Au, Pt, Pd from cleaned residue. Silver is recovered from HNO3 filtrate by NaCl precipitation.
descriptions.hno3_dilute: Selective silver and base metal recovery. Dilute HNO3 dissolves Cu, Ni, Pb and Ag while Au remains unreacted in the residue — available for further Aqua Regia processing.
descriptions.hno3_concentrated: Aggressive method for copper- and silver-rich feeds. Concentrated HNO3 rapidly dissolves base metals, Ag and Pd. Au and Pt remain in the residue for Aqua Regia follow-up. Requires NaCl to precipitate AgCl and zinc for silver reduction.
descriptions.hcl_h2o2: HCl/hydrogen peroxide acid etching — an Aqua Regia alternative that generates chlorine in-situ without NOx fumes. Dissolves Au and Pd; gold precipitated by SMB (sodium metabisulfite) and smelted with borax flux.
descriptions.nitrate_boat: Historical jeweller's refining method. Sodium nitrate + sulfuric acid generates nitric acid in-situ. Effective for cleaning gold alloys. Requires NaCl for AgCl precipitation and SMB to precipitate Au from solution.
descriptions.electrolysis: Selective electrolytic gold refining. Au transfers from anode to cathode as pure 999+ metal. Requires pre-melting feed into an anode with borax flux. Electrolyte: 10% HNO3 + gold nitrate Au(NO3)3.
descriptions.wohlwill_process: Industrial electrolytic process for highest gold purity (999.9). Gold chloride (AuCl3) in HCl electrolyte. Mint and refinery standard. Requires pre-melted anodes. Economical only for feeds with >90% Au.
descriptions.miller_process: Industrial pyrometallurgical gold refining by chlorine injection into molten metal in an induction furnace. Impurities (Ag, Cu, Pb, Zn) react with Cl2 forming chlorides that float as slag. Gold remains at 99.0–99.5%. Requires Cl2 scrubbing installation (NaOH). For certified refineries only.
descriptions.cementation_zinc: Classic gold cementation from solution. Zinc displaces Au/Ag from solution; gold precipitates as powder. Zinc then dissolved in dilute HCl, leaving clean gold/silver powder for smelting.

safety.aqua_regia: WARNING: Emits toxic NOx and HCl fumes. Work ONLY under active carbon-filtered ventilation. Equipment: ABEK P3 respirator, acid-resistant gloves (nitrile/neoprene), acid-resistant apron, safety goggles. Never add water to concentrated acid!
safety.hno3_dilute: Emits NO2/NO fumes (brown/colourless). Work under fume hood. Acid-resistant gloves required. During HCl stage: generates H2 gas on contact with zinc — no sparks!
safety.hno3_concentrated: HIGHLY DANGEROUS. Intense NOx fumes (brown smoke). Active carbon-filtered fume hood required. ABEK P3 respirator, acid-resistant gloves, goggles. Add feed very slowly in small portions — violent reaction!
safety.hcl_h2o2: No NOx fumes, but generates chlorine gas (yellow-green). Work under fume hood. Acid-resistant gloves, goggles. Add H2O2 very carefully in small portions — exothermic reaction!
safety.nitrate_boat: VERY HIGH TEMPERATURE + NOx fumes. Concentrated H2SO4 is violently exothermic with water — never add water to concentrated H2SO4! ABEK P3 respirator, heat-resistant acid-proof gloves, apron, goggles. Ventilation mandatory.
safety.electrolysis: Electrolysis itself is relatively safe. Electrolyte (10% HNO3) is corrosive — nitrile gloves and goggles required. Anode smelting stage requires burner (>1064°C) — use appropriate fire-protection equipment.
safety.wohlwill_process: Electrolyte at high temperature (65–70°C). HCl fumes — active ventilation mandatory. Acid-resistant gloves, goggles. Anode smelting requires burner (>1064°C) — thermal shield. AuCl3 is very expensive — only economical for high-purity feeds.
safety.miller_process: EXTREMELY DANGEROUS — chlorine gas is highly toxic (lethal at 430 mg/m³). REQUIRED: certified Cl2 scrubbing system with NaOH, full-face ABEK P3 respirator, acid-resistant suit, industrial ventilation. Minimum two-person operation. NOT for amateurs.
safety.cementation_zinc: CRITICAL if using NaCN — cyanides are highly toxic (lethal)! Always work at pH > 10 (alkaline). NEVER mix NaCN with acid (generates HCN gas!). HCl stage for zinc removal is safer but generates H2 — no sparks.

aqua_regia.0: Mechanical disassembly: remove screws, plastic housings, plug connectors, and large electrolytic capacitors (typically reduces feed mass by 20–40%)
aqua_regia.1: Material separation: remove plastic and ceramic parts (visual sorting), pull out ferromagnetics with a permanent magnet — they do not react with acids and impede filtration
aqua_regia.2: Feed shredding: cut PCBs into pieces < 2 cm with metal shears or a granulator — critical for speed and completeness of the acid reaction
aqua_regia.3: PPE: check fume hood, put on ABEK P3 respirator, acid-resistant gloves and goggles before starting
aqua_regia.4: HNO3 pre-etch: pour HNO3 25% over shredded feed (~1500 ml/kg active feed), heat to 40–50°C for 2–4 h — removes Cu, Ni, Pb, Fe, Sn; HNO3 is MANDATORY before aqua regia
aqua_regia.5: Filter and wash residue (Au, Pt, Pd, AgCl, ceramics) with distilled water — retain HNO3 filtrate for Ag and Cu recovery
aqua_regia.6: Aqua Regia: prepare mixture of HCl 35% + HNO3 65% in ratio 3:1 (vol.) in a PTFE or borosilicate glass vessel
aqua_regia.7: Slowly add residue to aqua regia, heat to 60–70°C stirring every 30 min — Au, Pt, Pd dissolve into solution
aqua_regia.8: After dissolution (4–8 h) filter off ceramics and AgCl — set AgCl aside for further silver recovery
aqua_regia.9: HNO3 decomposition: add urea CO(NH2)2 in portions (1–2 g/100 ml) until bubbling stops — destroys excess HNO3 which interferes with precipitation
aqua_regia.10: Au precipitation: slowly add SMB (NaHSO3) to cooled solution — gold precipitates as brown powder
aqua_regia.11: Filter gold powder, wash 3× with distilled water, wash with acetone, dry
aqua_regia.12: Smelting: place powder in graphite crucible, add pinch of borax flux, melt with torch or furnace (>1064°C)
aqua_regia.13: Precipitate Pt and Pd from filtrate — Pt via NH4Cl (precipitates (NH4)2PtCl6), Pd via DMAG or copper cementation

hno3_dilute.0: Mechanical disassembly: remove screws, plastic housings, plug connectors, and large electrolytic capacitors (typically reduces feed mass by 20–40%)
hno3_dilute.1: Material separation: remove plastic and ceramic parts (visual sorting), pull out ferromagnetics with a permanent magnet — they do not react with acids and impede filtration
hno3_dilute.2: Feed shredding: cut PCBs into pieces < 2 cm with metal shears or a granulator — critical for speed and completeness of the acid reaction
hno3_dilute.3: PPE: fume hood, acid-resistant gloves, safety goggles
hno3_dilute.4: Prepare HNO3 25% in an acid-resistant vessel (~1200 ml/kg active feed — critical quantity for full Cu/Ag/Ni dissolution)
hno3_dilute.5: Add electronic feed in portions — monitor reaction intensity (brown NO2 fumes)
hno3_dilute.6: Stir and heat to 40°C for 2–4 h — Cu, Ag, Ni, Pb dissolve into solution
hno3_dilute.7: Filter residue (Au, Pt, ceramics) — valuable feed for further aqua regia processing
hno3_dilute.8: Ag precipitation: add NaCl to filtrate — AgCl precipitates (white precipitate)
hno3_dilute.9: Filter AgCl, wash 3× with distilled water, dry
hno3_dilute.10: AgCl→Ag reduction: place AgCl in vessel, add Zn granules and cover with water — Zn displaces Ag from AgCl
hno3_dilute.11: Wait 30–60 min — filter grey Ag powder + Zn residue
hno3_dilute.12: Add dilute HCl — dissolves Zn, Ag remains; filter, wash and dry Ag powder
hno3_dilute.13: Smelt Ag powder in crucible — finished metallic silver

hno3_concentrated.0: Mechanical disassembly: remove screws, plastic housings, plug connectors, and large electrolytic capacitors (typically reduces feed mass by 20–40%)
hno3_concentrated.1: Material separation: remove plastic and ceramic parts (visual sorting), pull out ferromagnetics with a permanent magnet — they do not react with acids and impede filtration
hno3_concentrated.2: Feed shredding: cut PCBs into pieces < 2 cm with metal shears or a granulator — critical for speed and completeness of the acid reaction
hno3_concentrated.3: PPE: active carbon-filtered fume hood, ABEK P3 respirator, acid-resistant gloves, goggles
hno3_concentrated.4: Pour concentrated HNO3 65% into acid-resistant vessel (PTFE or borosilicate glass)
hno3_concentrated.5: Add feed VERY slowly in small portions — reaction is violent, intense brown NOx fumes
hno3_concentrated.6: Maintain temperature 30–40°C (exothermic reaction — cool if necessary)
hno3_concentrated.7: Continue until intense fumes cease (1–3 h per kg feed)
hno3_concentrated.8: Filter residue (Au, Pt, ceramics) — retain for further aqua regia processing
hno3_concentrated.9: Ag precipitation: add NaCl to filtrate — AgCl precipitates (white precipitate)
hno3_concentrated.10: Filter AgCl, wash 3× with distilled water, dry
hno3_concentrated.11: AgCl→Ag reduction: add Zn granules to AgCl suspension with water — Zn displaces Ag
hno3_concentrated.12: After 30–60 min filter grey Ag+Zn powder; add HCl — dissolves Zn, Ag remains
hno3_concentrated.13: Filter and wash Ag powder, smelt in crucible — finished metallic silver

hcl_h2o2.0: Mechanical disassembly: remove screws, plastic housings, plug connectors, and large electrolytic capacitors (typically reduces feed mass by 20–40%)
hcl_h2o2.1: Material separation: remove plastic and ceramic parts (visual sorting), pull out ferromagnetics with a permanent magnet — they do not react with acids and impede filtration
hcl_h2o2.2: Feed shredding: cut PCBs into pieces < 2 cm with metal shears or a granulator — critical for speed and completeness of the acid reaction
hcl_h2o2.3: PPE: fume hood (chlorine!), acid-resistant gloves, safety goggles
hcl_h2o2.4: Pour HCl 35% into acid-resistant vessel (PTFE or glass)
hcl_h2o2.6: Very slowly, in 10–20 ml portions, add H2O2 30% — chlorine will appear, reaction is exothermic
hcl_h2o2.7: Stir regularly, maintain temp. 40–50°C; add H2O2 when reaction slows
hcl_h2o2.8: After 12–24 h filter — ceramics and undissolved residue remain on filter
hcl_h2o2.9: Au precipitation: cool solution; slowly add SMB (NaHSO3) — brown Au powder precipitates
hcl_h2o2.10: Filter gold, wash 3× with distilled water, wash with acetone, dry
hcl_h2o2.11: Smelt Au powder in graphite crucible with a pinch of borax flux (>1064°C)

nitrate_boat.0: Mechanical disassembly: remove screws, plastic housings, plug connectors, and large electrolytic capacitors (typically reduces feed mass by 20–40%)
nitrate_boat.1: Material separation: remove plastic and ceramic parts (visual sorting), pull out ferromagnetics with a permanent magnet — they do not react with acids and impede filtration
nitrate_boat.2: Feed shredding: cut PCBs into pieces < 2 cm with metal shears or a granulator — critical for speed and completeness of the acid reaction
nitrate_boat.3: PPE: active carbon-filtered fume hood, ABEK P3 respirator, heat-resistant acid-proof gloves, goggles
nitrate_boat.4: Pour H2SO4 98% into a heat-resistant acid-proof vessel (quartz or niobate)
nitrate_boat.5: Gradually add NaNO3 to the acid — NOT the other way! Stir until dissolved
nitrate_boat.6: Heat to 80–100°C with continuous stirring — HNO3 forms in-situ
nitrate_boat.7: Add shredded electronic feed in portions
nitrate_boat.8: Maintain temperature 80–100°C for 3–6 h — metals dissolve into solution
nitrate_boat.9: Cool to room temperature; carefully dilute with water (STRONGLY EXOTHERMIC — add water TO acid, not the reverse!)
nitrate_boat.10: Ag precipitation: add NaCl to filtrate — white AgCl precipitate; filter, wash, dry
nitrate_boat.11: Au precipitation: add SMB (NaHSO3) to filtrate (AgCl-free) — brown Au powder precipitates
nitrate_boat.12: Filter gold, wash, dry and smelt in graphite crucible

electrolysis.0: Feed verification: check alloy composition (minimum 90% Au required) — use a touchstone, XRF spectrometer, or chemical assay
electrolysis.1: Pre-melting: if feed is powder or scrap, melt in a graphite crucible with borax flux and cast into preliminary ingots/anodes
electrolysis.2: Setup check: inspect electrical/gas equipment, calibrate meters, prepare full set of reagents and working vessel
electrolysis.3: PPE: nitrile gloves, goggles; for anode smelting — thermal shield and heat-resistant gloves
electrolysis.4: Anode smelting: melt feed in graphite crucible with borax flux — form anode (block or plate)
electrolysis.5: Prepare electrolyte: HNO3 10% + Au(NO3)3 in electrolytic cell, heat to 25–30°C
electrolysis.6: Mount anode (feed) and cathode (titanium foil or pure Au)
electrolysis.7: Set voltage 0.5–1.5 V, current density 50–200 A/m²
electrolysis.8: Electrolyse 12–48 h — gold deposits on cathode as pure 999+ metal
electrolysis.9: Regularly collect anode slime (Pt, Pd, Ag, others) for further processing
electrolysis.10: Remove cathode, clean of electrolyte, wash with distilled water, smelt
electrolysis.11: Result: 999+ purity gold; process anode slime by aqua regia or Wohlwill process

wohlwill_process.0: Feed verification: check alloy composition (minimum 90% Au required) — use a touchstone, XRF spectrometer, or chemical assay
wohlwill_process.1: Pre-melting: if feed is powder or scrap, melt in a graphite crucible with borax flux and cast into preliminary ingots/anodes
wohlwill_process.2: Setup check: inspect electrical/gas equipment, calibrate meters, prepare full set of reagents and working vessel
wohlwill_process.3: PPE: active ventilation (HCl fumes), acid-resistant gloves, goggles; for anode smelting — thermal shield
wohlwill_process.4: Anode smelting: melt feed (min. 90% Au) in graphite crucible with borax — form anodes (plates ~5–10 mm)
wohlwill_process.5: Prepare electrolyte: HCl 20% + AuCl3 10% in acid-resistant cell, heat to 65–70°C
wohlwill_process.6: Mount anodes and cathodes (titanium foil or pure Au)
wohlwill_process.7: Set parameters: U=1.0–1.2 V, current density 500–1000 A/m², T=65–70°C
wohlwill_process.8: Electrolyse 24–72 h — gold 999.9 deposits on cathodes as crystals
wohlwill_process.9: Remove cathodes, clean of electrolyte with distilled water, dry
wohlwill_process.10: Smelt cathodes in graphite crucible — finished gold 999.9
wohlwill_process.11: Anode slime (Pt, Pd, Ag) process separately (nitric acid or aqua regia)

miller_process.0: Feed verification: check alloy composition (minimum 90% Au required) — use a touchstone, XRF spectrometer, or chemical assay
miller_process.1: Pre-melting: if feed is powder or scrap, melt in a graphite crucible with borax flux and cast into preliminary ingots/anodes
miller_process.2: Setup check: inspect electrical/gas equipment, calibrate meters, prepare full set of reagents and working vessel
miller_process.3: PPE: certified Cl2 scrubber with NaOH, B2P3 filter mask or SCBA, acid-resistant suit, industrial ventilation — MINIMUM
miller_process.4: Start induction furnace, heat graphite crucible to 1100°C
miller_process.5: Add feed (gold alloy) and borax flux — wait until fully melted
miller_process.6: Connect Cl2 installation (quartz/clay tube) — check sealing of NaOH scrubber system
miller_process.7: Slowly blow Cl2 into molten metal — observe flame colour (yellow = AgCl, white = PbCl2/ZnCl2)
miller_process.8: Off-gas Cl2 passes through NaOH scrubber — check absorption with litmus paper
miller_process.9: Continue until coloured flames cease and molten gold clears (0.5–2 h)
miller_process.10: Stop Cl2 flow, pour golden metal into moulds — result: Au 99.0–99.5%
miller_process.11: Surface chlorides (slag) process for Ag recovery — they contain AgCl

cementation_zinc.0: Solution verification: check Au/Ag content of the input solution (e.g. after cyanide leaching or HNO3 filtrate)
cementation_zinc.1: pH adjustment: bring pH to 10–12 by adding Ca(OH)2 or NaOH — CRITICAL with NaCN (below pH 10 lethal HCN gas forms!)
cementation_zinc.2: Zinc granule preparation: rinse granules in dilute acid (removes oxides), wash with distilled water, prepare filtration container
cementation_zinc.3: PPE: for NaCN — B2P3 filter mask, nitrile gloves, fume hood; antidote (Na2S2O3) and first aid kit at hand
cementation_zinc.4: Add zinc granules (Zn) to alkaline Au solution — Au and Ag deposit on zinc (cementation)
cementation_zinc.5: Stir for 2–4 h — gold deposits on zinc as grey/brown precipitate
cementation_zinc.6: Filter precipitate (Au + Ag + Zn residue), wash with water
cementation_zinc.7: Zinc removal: transfer precipitate to vessel, add dilute HCl 35% — Zn dissolves, releases H2 (no sparks!); Au and Ag remain
cementation_zinc.8: Filter gold/silver powder, wash 3× with distilled water, dry
cementation_zinc.9: Smelt powder in graphite crucible with borax — finished Au/Ag alloy

analysis.disclaimerText: AI analysis results are estimates only and for informational purposes. Precious metal content can vary significantly by batch, manufacturer, and year of production. Laboratory assay is recommended before making purchase or process decisions. This result does not constitute technical advice or a recovery guarantee.
